key_insight: Using idealized sample geometry in inverse analysis can systematically underestimate the shear modulus of very soft tissue, especially in compression.
engineering_relevance: For reliable constitutive parameter identification in soft-tissue mechanics, the measured real geometry should be used instead of nominal idealizations.
method: MRI reconstructed the exact geometry of each brain tissue sample.
Finite element inverse analysis calibrated a shear modulus from tensile, compressive, and shear tests.
Results from real vs idealized geometries were directly compared under the same loading conditions.
evidence_signal: Across brain tissue tests, idealized geometries gave lower shear modulus estimates, with about 10% discrepancy in shear and about 48% under axial loading.
watch_item: Idealized geometry can misrepresent contact and strain fields, so using it in compression may introduce strong systematic bias.
paper_title: On the validity of using idealised sample geometries for interpreting mechanical tests of very soft tissues
